sentence: She came up to a brilliant idea during the brainstorming session.
correct: She came up with a brilliant idea during the brainstorming session.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <come up with> (not <come up to>). It means to think of an idea.

sentence: The company needs to crack down at tax evasion.
correct: The company needs to crack down on tax evasion.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <crack down on> (not <crack down at>). It means to take strict action.

sentence: He has been putting up to his neighbour's noise for months.
correct: He has been putting up with his neighbour's noise for months.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <put up with> (not <put up to>). It means to tolerate.

sentence: The detective was trying to piece up the evidence.
correct: The detective was trying to piece together the evidence.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <piece together> (not <piece up>). It means to assemble gradually.

sentence: She couldn't keep on with the pace of the advanced class.
correct: She couldn't keep up with the pace of the advanced class.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <keep up with> (not <keep on with>). It means to maintain the same level.

sentence: The government plans to phase off fossil fuel subsidies.
correct: The government plans to phase out fossil fuel subsidies.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <phase out> (not <phase off>). It means to gradually stop.

sentence: He managed to get away from cheating on the test.
correct: He managed to get away with cheating on the test.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <get away with> (not <get away from>). It means to avoid punishment.

sentence: The new regulations have done up with the old system.
correct: The new regulations have done away with the old system.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <do away with> (not <do up with>). It means to abolish.

sentence: She was looking forward at starting her new job.
correct: She was looking forward to starting her new job.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <look forward to> (not <look forward at>). Note: <to> + gerund.

sentence: He finally owned up for making the mistake.
correct: He finally owned up to making the mistake.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <own up to> (not <own up for>). It means to admit responsibility.

sentence: The manager asked us to cut down at unnecessary expenses.
correct: The manager asked us to cut back on unnecessary expenses.
The manager asked us to cut down on unnecessary expenses.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <cut back on> or <cut down on> (not <cut down at>).

sentence: We need to bear on mind that the deadline is approaching.
correct: We need to bear in mind that the deadline is approaching.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <bear in mind> (not <bear on mind>). It means to remember.

sentence: She talked him out from accepting the risky deal.
correct: She talked him out of accepting the risky deal.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <talk out of> (not <talk out from>). It means to persuade not to do.

sentence: The project fell apart because they couldn't see eye on eye.
correct: The project fell apart because they couldn't see eye to eye.
explanation: The correct idiom is <see eye to eye> (not <eye on eye>). It means to agree.

sentence: He was taken back by the unexpected news.
correct: He was taken aback by the unexpected news.
explanation: The correct expression is <taken aback> (not <taken back>). It means to be surprised.

sentence: She managed to pull off from organising the entire event alone.
correct: She managed to pull off organising the entire event alone.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <pull off> (not <pull off from>). It means to succeed in doing something difficult.

sentence: The new evidence brought up light on the mystery.
correct: The new evidence brought to light the mystery.
The new evidence shed light on the mystery.
explanation: The correct expressions are <bring to light> or <shed light on>.

sentence: He was let down from his colleagues during the crisis.
correct: He was let down by his colleagues during the crisis.
explanation: The correct preposition is <by> (not <from>) with <let down> in passive voice.

sentence: The company needs to branch up into new markets.
correct: The company needs to branch out into new markets.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <branch out> (not <branch up>). It means to expand.

sentence: She was banking at getting a promotion this year.
correct: She was banking on getting a promotion this year.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <bank on> (not <bank at>). It means to rely on.

sentence: The scandal blew up in the media and spiralled out from control.
correct: The scandal blew up in the media and spiralled out of control.
explanation: The correct expression is <out of control> (not <out from control>).

sentence: He was called up for his unprofessional behaviour.
correct: He was called out for his unprofessional behaviour.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <call out> (not <call up>). <Call out> means to criticise publicly.

sentence: The team needs to buckle up and focus on the deadline.
correct: The team needs to buckle down and focus on the deadline.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <buckle down> (not <buckle up>). <Buckle down> means to work hard.

sentence: She glossed up the mistakes in her report.
correct: She glossed over the mistakes in her report.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <gloss over> (not <gloss up>). It means to minimise or ignore.

sentence: The new policy has stamped up corruption in the department.
correct: The new policy has stamped out corruption in the department.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is <stamp out> (not <stamp up>). It means to eliminate.
